summary: McKenzie describes setting up CD fulfillment for Bingo Card Creator after his first physical media sale. He found a third-party CD fulfillment service that handles burning, labeling, and shipping for $4.47 per disc, charging customers $5 for the option. He explains the practical economics of physical media sales for a micro-ISV, including his policy of eating the cost on returns rather than processing physical returns. The post illustrates his pragmatic approach to solving business problems with minimal effort and maximum customer goodwill.
key_points: Some customers prefer physical media because it feels 'real' and serves as a backup, even when digital delivery is available
Third-party CD fulfillment services eliminate the need for a solo developer to handle physical logistics like burning, labeling, and shipping
At $4.47 cost versus $5 price, the CD option is essentially break-even after accounting for time spent processing orders
For low-value physical items, absorbing the cost of returns rather than processing them is better economics and better customer service
money_quotes: A lot of people who aren't the most technologically-savvy in the world prefer to have physical media, as its a) 'real' in a way the download is not and b) proof against something going wrong on their computer.

Its certainly not worth sending me out to the video store to buy a CD or a stack of CDs, hand-write a license key on it, and then trudge it out to the post office, buy a padded mailer, and ship it abroad to the US, praying it doesn't get a 25% tariff or get dropped in the freaking ocean like the last CD I sent to America.

About ten minutes of research later I signed up with them, sent them a CD image over the Interweb, and now every time I get an order I just have to log into my account, fill out the order details, and hit 'Send'.
How do you handle a return of a CD? Answer: you don't.
There is no point even thinking about going to the post office for a five dollar item, and your customers will appreciate it immensely.
If a customer buys the CD and wants to use the 30-day guarantee, I will tell them to please dispose of the CD and I will take the loss ($4.47).
key_insight: When selling low-cost physical goods, outsource fulfillment to eliminate logistics headaches and absorb return costs rather than process them — the customer goodwill is worth more than the few dollars lost.
